Bc4 White develops their bishop to increase control over d5 and pressure f7, the weakest pawn in Black's position. The Bishop's opening is very transpositional: by not having played Nf3 yet, White retains the option of f4 and may plan to transpose into Vienna or King's gambit lines, or the game can be taken into a Russian or Italian direction.
e5 is the Open game (or the Double King's Pawn game). It is Black's classical response to 1.

variations.name: Vienna Hybrid, Spielmann Attack

annotation.moveRationale: d3 reinforces the center solidly, maintaining flexibility in the Bishop's Opening.

White has nailed down Black's d-pawn by controlling d5 with three pieces. This prevents Black from breaking open the centre, or does it.
As White, you still have designs on playing f4, but Black with their knight sortie has challenged your control of d5. Should you fight back, or ignore this distraction.

White's wish is to advance the f-pawn two squares to remove Black's powerful e5 pawn and strike at the heart of their position. Now it is possible to play f4 on the second move, for the King's Gambit, but when no Black piece has yet declared its intentions is there not a degree of hit-and-hope about such a move.
e5 is the Open game (or the Double King's Pawn game). It is Black's classical response to 1.

annotation.moveRationale: d5 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Bishop's Opening.
Rather than recover the pawn, White invites Black to trade knights. White's plan after 4.
Black takes the undefended e4 pawn with 3. Now it would be unwise to continue with 4.
This move is usually played by beginners, who are used to the Italian Game, however this does leave the e4 pawn hanging. Black can take the undefended e4 pawn with 3.
Rather than defend e5, Black counterattacks e4. This is the Russian game, aka Petrov's or Petroff's defence, a solid if drawish opening.
White develops a piece to a more active square, asserts control in the centre and over the d4 square, and attacks Black's e5-pawn. This is the most common opening played in chess.
e5 is the Open game (or the Double King's Pawn game). It is Black's classical response to 1.
